title: Dog Red Inflamed Ear Flap: Causes and Care Guide
meta_desc: Is your dog's ear red and inflamed? Learn common causes, urgency levels, and how a photo helps your vet provide the best care for your pet.
content_html:
TL;DR: Red, inflamed skin inside a dog's ear usually indicates otitis or dermatitis and requires a veterinary exam within 24-48 hours. Sending a photo to your vet can help them assess the urgency and provide immediate comfort recommendations.

What causes red and inflamed skin inside my dog's ear?

When the skin inside a dog's ear flap (the pinna) appears red, swollen, or angry, it is a clinical sign of inflammation often referred to as otitis or dermatitis. This condition is frequently caused by underlying issues such as yeast or bacterial infections, seasonal allergies, ear mites, or even a foreign object like a grass seed trapped in the canal. The redness is often accompanied by heat, itching, and discomfort.

Is red, inflamed skin inside my dog's ear a veterinary emergency?

  • Inflammation of the ear flap is generally classified with a Medium urgency level.
  • While rarely life-threatening, it is often very painful and can lead to complications like a ruptured eardrum or an aural hematoma if the dog shakes its head excessively.
  • You should aim to have your pet seen by a veterinarian within 24 to 48 hours, especially if there is a foul odor or discharge.

How can a photo of my dog's ear help the veterinarian with triage?

  • A clear, well-lit photo allows the clinical team to assess the severity of the swelling.
  • Visuals help identify specific types of discharge, such as dark waxy debris versus yellow pus.
  • Photos help determine if the inflammation is localized or spreading across the pinna.
  • The information allows the clinic to prioritize your appointment and provide immediate comfort measures while you wait for your visit.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Arthropods, such as ticks, can cause dermatitis of the pinna through direct damage from bites or hypersensitivity. Specifically, the spinous ear tick (Otobius megnini) can parasitize the external ear canal of dogs, leading to head shaking, head rubbing, or drooped pinnae. Treatment involves mechanical removal of ticks and ivermectin administration. Allergies (environmental or food-induced atopic dermatitis) are also common causes of erythema and pruritus of the pinnae and external ear canals in dogs, predisposing them to secondary bacterial or yeast otitis externa, potentially extending to the pinna with papules, crusts, and lichenification in addition to the erythema. Diagnosis is based on history, clinical signs, and elimination of other pruritic skin diseases, including a strict food elimination trial. Primary causes of otitis externa can cause inflammation in a normal ear and can be subtle; they often go unrecognized until secondary causes develop. Major primary causes of otitis externa include allergy, autoimmune disease, endocrine disorders, epithelialization disorders, foreign bodies, glandular disorders, immune-mediated reactions, fungal infections, parasites, and viral infections.

Chapter: Dermatology, Parasitology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 520)

faqs.question: What are common signs of an ear infection in dogs?
faqs.answer: Common signs include frequent head shaking, scratching at the ear, a foul odor, discharge, and visible redness or swelling of the ear flap.

faqs.question: Can I clean my dog's red ear at home?
faqs.answer: You should consult your vet before cleaning a highly inflamed ear. If the eardrum is ruptured, some cleaners can cause permanent damage or deafness.

faqs.question: Why does my dog's ear feel hot to the touch?
faqs.answer: Increased temperature in the ear flap is a sign of inflammation and increased blood flow to the area, usually caused by an infection or an allergic reaction.
